777 North First SEYECC

500
San JOSS,
California 95112
Dear Mr. rezzymnn:
Thank you for your recent letter
in which You d13-
cussed our hearing On the rxanchiso Tax Board
audlgs.
As you may haw: heard, this hearinn an: postponed and
rescheduled for November
3;
1977.
since your letter make: haven-1 concrete suqqel~
rims on how to not only Lrnpzove the. Audits but also
the nautical harem \ct,
I would hope that you would
accept Out invitation to address the chlsﬁion at
the
‘lovember meetmg.
In any cue, I win iorvnxd your letter to thu
Comanche”.
Thank you (a: taking the time ta express you:
VLBWE and
I lack forward
to hﬁdting
from yuu whether
you can ntbend our meclng.
Sincerely,
Robert H. San
General Counsal
FHSXHC


s
A.
swam...
.
\x
w
5268 Scotts Valley or.
m as"; “new."
”ARL'” 4' FER‘VM‘”
W
5?" sameness:
“"7333. .52...:: (Ana)
Fair Political
Practices Camisslon
1100 Kay street
Sacramento. on 95814
Attention: Robert Stein
Gentlemen:
l have read in your bulletin notiCe that the FPPC intends to
hold a hearing concerning the auditing procedures of the Franchise
Tax Board re lobbyists and campaign committees.
it seems peculiar
that such heating would be Called in View of the FPPC'S role
in the Governor-s striking of the control language in the
appropriation pill for the ma which dealt with audits.
souever.
as a campaign treasurer who has been audited twice,
I wlsh to
submit my comments because 1 do not relish completing anothez
questionably effective questionnaire which deals with the prob-
lems of the political reform act.
First.
I wish to address myself to the "confirmation letters" »
and their effect on both contributors and treasurers.
when the
lettexs are sent out concernlng our campaign committee,
my law
office is deluged with phone calls regarding these letters.
The
majority of calls request that i give them the data necessary to
fill out the form because they are not sure of haw many fund
raising functions they have attended at when.
In order to avoid
alienating a contributoz,
1 have been informing them as to what
my records show, and as tactfully as possible, I explain that
their conduct is of worthless effect because the FTE is checking
on me and they want independent information.
a minority of
callers are irate about the inquiry and ask if they can throw
the letter in the waste-basket and they are tired of
furnishing
anything to a job»making bureauracy or something to that effect.
The confirmation letters present another problem with small
contributors because of their problem in determining who is the
donor.
For example, donor buys a ticket to a dinner for «550.00
paying from his personal account.
he is the owner of a small
business which contributes 550.00 to the campaign.
Donor-s wife
then executes a check for 550.00 for another fund raiser
which
is drawn on the couple's joint bank account.
Donor is frequently


of the opinion that he has contributed 3150.00 personally instead
of the 350.00 and will report thusly unless it is explained to
him.
In no case, has anyone which your entity has labelled the
"special interests" contacted me concerning confirmation letters.
They obviously understand the law and its procedures.
calls to
me originate with the element of
contributuzs whlch has barely
broken the reporting threshold.
1 oppose the sending of any questionnaire to persons who have
been audited as a waste of time and money.
I have completed one
ouestionaire which I considered to be a waste of time because there
was no way in it to address the real limitations of the political
Reform Act.
In many cases the right questions did not appear and
when relevant ones did there was a lack of space to be specific.
It seemed to me that the effect of the questionnaire was to
demonstrate what a fine job the buzeaucratlc agencies were doing.
In some cases, certain departments are doing a good job, but 1
for one would like to explain how and why.
I have no complaint about the types of violations listed in
the audit reports, probably because of since the time that 1
became treasurer there have been none.
The FTE corrected its
earlier policy regarding lobbyists4
reports eliminating its
nit' picking policyi
The principal problem here seems to me to
be in the enforcement policy.
I note in the Andersen a Company
report to the Joint Legislative Audit Committee that the Commission
has not completed investigation of 31 of the 32 audit reports
which indicated substantial noncompliance.
I presume that these
principally involved mandatory audits.
if your agency is interested
in a real mess, perhaps you should randomly check the reports of
local candidates.
out of curiosity i have checked several of
these reports.
R considerable number indicate substantial
violations on their face.
it seems here that a committee for
a local candidate is really on the honor system.
I have frequently
been contacted by anirious treasurers for these committees regarding
reporting problems,
and have tried to help them.
However,
I
realized that they were safe unless an opponent made a complaint
because otherwise they would never undergo a real audit.
I realize that all agencies connected with enforcement will
raise the excuse that the legislature has not provided sufficient
funds for enforcement.
Assuming for the sake of argument that
more funds are warranted for enforcement, it seems unllkely that
the Legislature would be foolish enough to provide for more funds
for enforcement which would be substantially used against the
member's own campaign committees.
They have enough txcuble keeping
treasurers themselves.
It seems that a random auditing procedure such as the Legislature
enacted in its control language in the no appropriation would be
the answer to many problems.
I wish them luck in the litigation
concerning this.
That audit
procedure wuuld Certainly cut dawn
.2-


on the time that a treasurer has to spend with the auditors each
year.
1 have undergone two audits which required me to schedule
several days of
time
t!) be available to
ensue: the questions of
the auditors.
After
a short time both auditors.
I believe realized
that they were not going to find anything that was not adequately
documented,
but nevertheless because of
the existing procedures,
they had to audit laboriously from A to 2 my bulky committee
records.
If
the
Internal. Revenue service audited each and every
return filed,
the tax system in this country would be wrecked.
I oppose vehemently any extension of the two-year statute
of
limitations.
First it is already twice as long as that far
most misdemeanars under California law,
but even more seriously.
prosecutions would be against people who are largely volunteers
and amateurs guilty of inadvertent violations.
I can readily
foresee that any flurry of enforcement activity on your part would
largely effect campaign treasurers.
The treasurer-s control over
a campaign apparatus is limited in any case.
He is held responsible
for the financial activities Of
a motley army of unpaid and
transient Volunteers who know little about the Political Reform
Act and in some cases Care less.
It is apparent that the drafters
Of
the act did not understand how political committees function
or they would not have applied draconian standards to such
non-business like entities.
It seems only fair that
a treasure:
or campaign worker should be able to rest in peace two years
after quiting politics.
I have attempted above only to address my comments to the
matters coming up in your hearing.
However,
I wish to make this
comment on the reform act generally.
In a very substantial number
of instances, it has acted against the very people that it was
intended to help.
it is an lncumbents‘ act pure and simple in
its results.
It has discouraged amateurs and volunteers because
of fear.
it is extremely expenslve and worrying to any new
candidate who desires to run for anything.
I hope that any
amendments t0 improve the act come from someone besides the
drafters of the act and its sponsors.
They have adequately
demonstrated tu me that they
d0 DOC understand the political
presses.
sincerely yours,
eff/(LMWRQJ ww/
Harlin J.
Fern/mgr:
HJP/j

sections.question_synthetic: The document is not a question-answer format; rather it is a letter from Harlan J. Perryman, a campaign treasurer, submitting comments to the FPPC regarding the Franchise Tax Board's auditing procedures and the effectiveness of the Political Reform Act.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document is OCR-scanned incoming correspondence, not an FPPC advice letter. Contains no Q/C sections. The first page is a brief acknowledgment letter from Robert Stein (FPPC General Counsel) dated October 21, 1977, forwarding Perryman's substantive letter to the Commission. The main document is Perryman's detailed comments on audit procedures and political reform compliance. Some OCR artifacts present but content generally legible.

embedding.summary: This is an incoming correspondence from campaign treasurer Harlan J. Perryman to the FPPC expressing concerns about FTB auditing procedures, confirmation letter requirements, audit questionnaires, and the enforcement of the Political Reform Act. He advocates for random auditing procedures and opposes extending the statute of limitations.
